one_line_takeaway: A text-only, MCP-compatible malicious tool server can steer LLM agents into long multi-turn tool-calling chains that inflate per-query cost by up to 658x and energy by up to ~561x while keeping the final answer correct and evading content-based defenses, exposing the agent-tool interface as an unmonitored economic-DoS surface.
key_finding: Across six LLMs and two benchmarks, the attack produces trajectories exceeding 60,000 output tokens per query while keeping attack success rate (ASR) close to the benign task success rate (TSR). Per-query cost is amplified by up to 658.10x (Mistral-Large on BFCL: 57,255 vs. 87 tokens), with the smallest amplification still 65.51x (Seed-32B on ToolBench). Energy rises ~100-560x (e.g., Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct on ToolBench: 5.63 Wh -> 3159.45 Wh, x561.18) and peak GPU KV-cache occupancy jumps from <1% benignly to 35-74% under attack. Concurrent benign-workload throughput is roughly halved (avg ~50% drop; up to 64.6% for Seed-32B on ToolBench, 4001 -> 1417 tokens/s). Critically, standard defenses fail: a perplexity (PPL) input filter stays within the benign envelope (ineffective), and self-monitoring plus Qwen-Guard-3 / Llama-Guard-3 trajectory judges flag the attack below 3% (near 0%) while strongly flagging Overthink baselines. Hard per-session token caps and tool-call limits merely throttle worst-case cost but do not detect or stop the attack, as it adapts to consume the allowed budget. MCTS optimization matters: on ToolBench/Qwen it raises the hand-crafted template's 51.3% ASR / 37,425 tokens to 80.5% ASR / 65,273 tokens.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a tool-layer, multi-turn economic denial-of-service (DoS) attack against LLM agents operating under the Model Context Protocol (MCP). Unlike prior single-turn DoS attacks that operate at the user-prompt or RAG-context layer (Engorgio, P-DoS, Auto-DoS, Overthink), this work turns a benign, MCP-compliant tool server into a malicious drop-in variant by editing ONLY text-visible fields (argument descriptions, in-progress/corrective messages) while leaving function signatures, identifiers, and the terminal benign payload unchanged. The malicious template steers the agent into prolonged, verbose tool-calling chains (many turns, each with long output) yet still returns the correct final answer, making it 'correctness-preserving.' The authors claim this is the first work to treat the tool-calling step as a first-class DoS attack surface, and they contribute a universal, payload-preserving malicious template plus an MCTS-based optimizer that discovers effective templates under black-box constraints.
threat_model: Adversary controls (or impersonates) an MCP tool server Tθ and may replace its benign template θ0 with a text-only variant θ, remaining protocol-compatible. The attacker has ONLY black-box access to the agent policy A and the underlying LLM M, and cannot modify A, M, user queries, prompts, or retrieval. The attack activates only after the tool is legitimately invoked under the benign configuration. The adversary's goal is a constrained optimization: maximize expected output-token cost C(τ)=Tok_out(τ) subject to preserving task success with probability at least p_min. The harm is economic (inflated API/token cost, energy, GPU KV-cache pressure, reduced concurrency/throughput) rather than semantic toxicity or incorrect answers, which is precisely why standard content-safety defenses miss it.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) evaluation is in a controlled agent emulator with deterministic, stubbed tool payloads, so results may differ in production agent stacks and under different runtimes/hardware; (2) they focus only on the single-tool, single-turn subsets of ToolBench/BFCL for comparability, not multi-tool long-horizon tasks; (3) the template relies on a structured numeric 'calibration sequence' to elicit long outputs, and other protocol-compliant long-form content patterns are not exhaustively explored. Additional reviewer-noted caveats: the Overthink baseline is adapted to a non-RAG setting (decoy moved into the user query), which may understate its native strength; the attacker components use gpt-4o for one-shot rewriting and Llama-3.3-70B as the MCTS Editor, so reproducibility depends on those models; and the strongest amplification factors are partly a function of very small benign token baselines (e.g., 87 tokens for Mistral-Large/BFCL).

datasets_used: ToolBench (Fan et al. 2025, MCPToolBench++) - filtered to single-turn/single-tool prompts: 105 MCP servers, 261 queries
BFCL - Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard (Patil et al. 2025) - filtered subset: 80 MCP servers, 203 queries
quantitative_results: Max cost amplification 658.10x: Mistral-Large on BFCL (57,255 vs 87 output tokens)
314.73x cost amplification: Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct on ToolBench (81,830 vs 260 tokens)
Trajectories exceed 60,000 output tokens per query; e.g. Llama on ToolBench 81,830 tokens (96.2% ASR vs 98.1% benign TSR), on BFCL 77,052 tokens (93.9% ASR)
Energy amplification ~100-560x: Llama-3.3-70B on ToolBench 5.63 Wh -> 3159.45 Wh (x561.18); Mistral-Large on BFCL 4.24 Wh -> 2269.60 Wh (x535.28)
Peak GPU KV-cache occupancy: <1% benign -> 35-74% under attack (Mistral-Large/ToolBench 0.4% -> 73.9%; Llama/BFCL 0.3% -> 66.1%)
Concurrent benign throughput roughly halved (~50% avg drop); worst case Seed-32B/ToolBench 4001 -> 1417 tokens/s (64.6% drop)
MCTS vs hand-crafted on ToolBench/Qwen: ASR 51.3% -> 80.5%, tokens 37,425 -> 65,273
Overthink single-turn cap: at most ~10^4 tokens on non-RAG setting (e.g. 8,743 on Qwen-3-32B/ToolBench)

Defense evasion: self-monitoring, Qwen-Guard-3, and Llama-Guard-3 flag the attack <3% (near 0%); PPL filter stays within benign docstring envelope; token caps / tool-call limits throttle but do not stop (ASR stays high)
Serving cap M = 16,384 max completion tokens per generation; concurrency fixed at 25 queries on 8x H200 GPUs
baselines_compared: Benign MCP server (no attack) - correctness baseline (TSR)
Overthink (ICL-Genetic, Agnostic; Kumar et al. 2025) - single-turn, decoy trigger placed in user query since benchmarks are non-RAG
Overthink-MT - multi-turn extension of Overthink using 6 tool calls to match budget, trigger kept at context layer, tool server left benign
Hand-crafted template - text-only, protocol-compatible, payload-preserving template WITHOUT MCTS optimization
